speaker: interviewer
text: Today is Tuesday, February the 11th, 1997. I'm Shirley Murgraff interviewing Lothar Singer in London, England. And the interview is in English. Today is Tuesday, February the 11th, 1997. I'm Shirley Murgraff interviewing Lothar Singer in London, England. Can you tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lothar Singer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-O-T-H-A-R S-I-N-G-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: 5th of December, 1919.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Czernowitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-Z-E-R-N-O-W-I-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was Czernowitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czernowitz was in the Bukowina part, before the First World War, [INAUDIBLE]. After the-- or between the two world wars, Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Bukowina?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-U-K-O-W-I-N-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your early childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: My early childhood was a happy one in a Jewish middle-class household. My father was a chartered engineer, textile branch. My late mother was in the house, all preparing for us, and living for her family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your grandmother live with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I did not know both grandmothers. They died before or-- I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your first school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember your first day I went to kindergarten. That was a private kindergarten. The director, a Mr. Dubenski, was a school-friend of my late grandfather. My mother was also a pupil of his. And she brought me over, presented me. He had his new pupil. I learned German, French, Hebrew, and also Romanian.

speaker: interviewer
text: In kindergarten?

speaker: interviewee
text: In kindergarten.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you started kindergarten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five, approximately.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember celebrating Jewish festivals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. My parents were traditional. And we kept all the-- kosher in the household and tradition in Jewish household.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that with affection? Do you have any especially good memories about any of those celebrations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, because we looked forward to Hanukkah, Purim, and to-- and going to shul, with the parents, and, afterwards, visiting my paternal grandfather in his shul, because he was in [INAUDIBLE], he was a [INAUDIBLE] in the shul-- to used to visit him in the interval.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have one sister, seven and half years younger than I.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your early kindergarten days, then, still? Was it hard work? Did you enjoy it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I enjoyed it, in particular the-- the girls. I was always sitting with the girls. We were singing in Hebrew and in-- in German, learning rhymes. I was interested.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a Jewish kindergarten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mixed, but not many Gentiles, mostly Jewish. You have to pay. It was a private kindergarten.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the non-Jewish pupils learn Hebrew, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it unusual for Jewish families to send their children to such private education then?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, the-- let us call the academically-trained parent tried to convey to their children the knowledge which they acquired. And so we learned from the early age. In particular my father-- late father was-- had this interest in languages. He, himself, spoke many languages. And he wanted me also to follow. He always said, any language is a tool in the struggle for life.

speaker: interviewer
text: You talked of celebrating Jewish festivals. Did you go to-- did your family go to a synagogue, regularly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very, not every sabbat, Shabbat. But the Holy Days, these were strictly kept. And Shabbat, of course, never permitted to write and to go on the bike, if we had one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you always light candles on Friday evenings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What school did you go to next after your kindergarten and how old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the age of six, six and a half, started the Meisler Schule, again, a private school, which-- high renown, very-- Again, I learned foreign languages. German was the main language, French, Hebrew, and Romanian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy that school, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the proportion of Jewish students, in that school, would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: 90%. There were very few non-Jewish children, which were from the gentile elite. In my class, there were three, two girls and one boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would have been the gentile elite in those days?

speaker: interviewee
text: The boy, which I sat together-- 12 years, in the school, we went together. He was a son of the attorney general. And the girls, one was the daughter of a very renowned chemist, [? Monika ?] [? Fontaine, ?] I remember. He had a big shop, and he was from the German elite. And then another girl, her father had a big haulage business, a rich man, houses, properties.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how many pupils in your class at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: 20-odd.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the proportion of Jews in the town of Czernowitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were about 40,000 Jews in a population of 80,000, probably 50% or more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a prosperous sort of town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made it prosperous?

speaker: interviewee
text: Industry. It has a university. In the same way it was now in trade, because it was on the border, not far from the border with Poland and not far to Czechoslovakia. So it was trade went through this, import and export, went through the town. It has a very, really developed textile industry, mostly Jewish owners. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Timber export. Agricultural products export. And generally, [INAUDIBLE]. They had, also, poor Jews, which were mostly, let us call, in a sort of Judengasse as it was. It was where the old synagogue was a large building. And there were the poorer Jews. That also-- the town had a very rich hinterland, which all came to buy in Czernowitz, which, again, added to the trade. We were the largest town in the region. The second largest after-- in Romania after Bucharest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home, mainly?

speaker: interviewee
text: German, mostly German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the main language in Czernowitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: German. They had to learn Romanian under punishment. If you spoke another language than Romanian, it was punished. You had to pay a penalty. But most of the elderly people did not know Romanian, so they paid their penalty and continued, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak other languages at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, English. I was a member of the Franco-Romanian Club and the Anglo-Romanian Club. I had the occasion to speak and to use their libraries and their newspapers and to get accustomed to the customs of the country. We had always very known speakers coming from abroad and courses for English, courses for French. So we used it. These were 90% Jewish students.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said your sister was born 7 and 1/2 years after you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight.

speaker: interviewer
text: --come as a great shock? I mean were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not. I was glad to have a sister. Because the others-- my friends had brothers or sisters, you know? And I wanted, always, particularly a sister. And when she was born, she was like my possession.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the beginning, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you got on with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her starting school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She started going, as a little girl, in a state school, because this was in [NON-ENGLISH]. In the meantime, we had moved and started with another girl, a [? Renee ?] [? Silver. ?] In the same house, they were living, and they started school together. They always used to go together to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- what year was she born?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, towards the end of the '20s, feeling any effects, in your economic situation, from the economic situation in the rest of the world then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember my father had a factory in one of the houses of my grandfather. And which he had to sell, because of the crash in 1927. And then he started and worked for a German firm, Rudolf Bannasch and Company, which had its main headquarter in Tarutino and Bessarabia. They had, all over the country, stores or selling. My late father was first technical director. And afterwards, with the sale and distribution of the product, because they produced from the wool up to the cloth, already cloth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that affect your home economic circumstances?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, we had to reduce the life which we had before. But it was better than being unemployed.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were aware of people being unemployed in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes, unfortunately.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that-- sorry. Carry on.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, we tried always, my late mother, in particular, was active in the charity work, particularly Jewish. So sometimes I went with her to see how other people lived. She always went to the old peoples' home. And it was like, let us call, equivalent as a WIZO here. Make collection in the early age, I was flag day and I was collecting money for the Jewish orphanage, we had. It was, in general, a very, very, well-organized Jewish community, there. We had school, Jewish school, from the primary school, elementary school, up to grammar school. We had our Jewish hospital. We had Jewish orphanage, Jewish-- More or less, every society, if you need a dole for a poor girl, so my mother and my aunt were busy. And this helped. We were well known in the town. My grandfather was 48 year town councilor. Also, his constituency, if you could call it so, was mostly non-Jewish or gentile. It was in the suburb, where he grew up and his brothers. And so we were well-known. As a matter of fact, on every church in our town, the name of my grandfather was as founder. He was a Jew, a religious Jew of his birth. He didn't go to church. But his worker came to him. He had the forest exploitation, timber exploitation and stone exploitations. They asked for help to build their church. So take what you need, God will give me back. That's what he always said.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that unusual, for the time, in that part of Romania?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell that it was unusual, because we had a good relationship between the local population, gentile population. It started when the Romanian-- after the Romanian occupied it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1919, when the Austria-Hungarian Empire was dismembered. And Bukowina became part of Romania. Then the Romanian Army entered and started looting and started asking for baksheesh, that is. And that became-- they knew how to cover it. The Jews sent their MPs, I remember, in parliament, we had Strauher, I remember, and Ebner, Dr. Ebner and Dr. Strauher. Dr. Strauher was also a school-friend of my late grandfather. And they acted for-- in the parliament. And Dr. Pistener, which was a lifelong friend of my late father. He was a Social Democrat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father politically active?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He studied in Charlottenburg, in Berlin. There he got influenced by [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh, what's it called? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The existing social democracy in bloom, before the war, which was prohibited, really, by the Prussian officer corps and so on. But he was-- continued afterwards-- he-- when he came to Czernowitz. So he was always a social democrat. I remember the 1st of May, there was a demonstration, a march through the town. We always went there. And I collected the insignia for every year I had.

speaker: interviewee
text: You collected the?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had insignias, small insignia. So I collected them for every year I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it your father's father who was a councilor for so long?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my mother's father, maternal grandfather. My father's father, paternal grandfather, before the war, he had estate, with mill, forest. And he had, when the Romanian came in, they decorated him, with a high insignia, because his neighbor was Jancu Flondor, who became the minister for Bukowina, was an estate owner. They grew up, my father, with his daughter, grew up together. Afterwards, she married the minister of finance in Romania, Cancicov. And I remember, always to new year, they used to send a bottle of wine from their estate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of any anti-semitism in those early years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in school, really, and not on the street. You see, in the street, where we lived, were Romanian academics Our-- next-door was a high court judge, Stefaneli. [NON-ENGLISH] Stefaneli was his son. He was a friend of mine. Also, he was older. We shared the same hobby. Opposite from us was Baron von Anauch, whose wife was from the British Royalty. And we did not have, really, to deal with the ordinary Romanian.

speaker: interviewer
text: The policeman was respectful, because my grandfather brought him up. He was an orphan. He grew up. And Diverse was our police officer, you see? So it was this-- everybody knew us and respected the family. What year did you leave your primary school, your elementary school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After? I had to pass entrance examination. And I-- during this summer, I passed with top marks. Coming from the private school, you see, I had a better preparation than the others, which came from state school. I was-- I was supposed to go to the Realschule Number 2. But in-- during the vacation, the school was closed for budgetary reasons. So the top few, which were mostly Jews, were sent to the Realschule Number 1, which was a grant-aided school by the Romanian Orthodox Church. We were the first Jews, in group, to come in the school. But after a year, most were thrown out. In the fifth year-- it was a grammar school. In Romania, you had eight years grammar school. In the fifth year, there were only seven left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven-- the other were all-- or they went alone or they were pushed out. It was anti-semitism. There it was. Between my professors-- see, I had the top of the Iron Guard, Professor Tsopa. He was a chief of the Bukowina, of the Iron Guard. And Professor Osietuk, who was a-- on the senate of the Legion, the Iron Guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: I personally cannot complain, because I was always helpful. I made sports, like the gentile. I-- they knew my parents, the professors knew them or a friend with my uncles or school colleagues, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I cannot complain that I was-- I was sitting in the same-- the front bench, with my colleague, Kornel Vlad, which later, also, became attorney general. And a colleague, who was, afterwards, the head of the Hitlerjugend. But he was of Jewish parentage. His father was Jewish. And he became Catholic. But it didn't help him. He was shot by the Russians when the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: You talked of your uncles. Did both your parents come from largish families?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were really cousins second-grade. That-- so that I have my-- the same family, really. They had-- my mother had two brothers-- three brothers and one sister, alive in my time. And my father had, also, four brothers, about four brothers and one sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they at a similar economic level to you with their families?

speaker: interviewee
text: More or less. You see, my uncle, the oldest uncle, was the owner of an estate, ex-yeshiva boy. The second was accountant in a big firm. And the youngest lived in southern Bukowina. He had his own business there. By the way, he was killed before the war by a Romanian in a village he went to. And he was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a court case about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a court case, but they do nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you saying he was killed because he was a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they went after money. They were thinking he had money. You see, the husband of my aunt, from paternal-side, had-- was a supplier for the army. He had a factory, but he was a supplier for the army. And he could not go with soldiers to collect some hay, so he asked my uncle, which had moved to Czernowitz, perhaps he would go in his stead. He went. And after they collected, the soldier collect, on the carriages, the hay, he went to pay the restaurant what they have eaten there. And the returning, the mayor of the village made an ambush and killed him with an axe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what year that was, approximately?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have many cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in contact with them quite a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, we were. We all went to school and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your hobbies?

speaker: interviewee
text: My hobbies? Reading, music, and stamp collecting.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying, earlier, that your first direct experience of anti-semitism was in your secondary, your grammar school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Apart from that, did you find the studies stimulating and were you a good student?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to be, because, if not, they would have thrown me out as a Jew. It was an elite school. And they kept only the best of the Jews, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was helped. We got everything from the school, books and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what we needed-- a fantastic library. They had a good museum in the school. It was an elite school, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Romanian Orthodox Church was very rich. They had estates and estates and housing. And they founded this school-- funded this school very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was-- we were all in uniform. And it was a sort of military lines. You see, in the morning, they could pull up the-- the flag and let it down at 1 o'clock.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the whole week. It was military. They knew my late father was officer in the reserve. For that, I applied also. I had colleagues, which were former colleagues of his in the army. that had an influence, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it only a boys' school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only boys. We had a sister school, which was also grant-aided, grant-aided by the Romanian Legionary Front. We were not permitted to go with other girls than our-- from our sister school, which we learned dancing, under supervision, of course. And they did not know, really, to discern if I'm Jewish or not. I spoke Romanian, with Romanian accent. And most, for the intelligentsia, spoke German, which was my mother tongue. It came when the younger came, which were already fascist, and the German, because I was in the Hitler time, mostly, in the grammar school. Then you started feeling the influence. Of Some of my colleagues had the swastika under the-- and a Romanian design. All were connected with the youth movement, the fascists of the Iron Guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of the teachers Jewish in that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had, a short time, a teacher for a rabbi, Dr. Schulzon. I think we came once a week. Then a cousin of my mother, who was professor of philosophy and history, he gave lessons. He was a colleague of many of the gentile professors. But when Hitler started in Germany, so they didn't let the Jewish lessons. First, we had in the park, opposite the school. And then we had a priest, who taught us and examined us, in order to give us-- And that was our Jewish education.

speaker: interviewer
text: Coming to the end of this tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
